title-group.article-title: Efficacy, immunogenicity, and safety of BCG revaccination against Mycobacterium tuberculosis: A systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Medicine, Liaquat University of Medical and Health Sciences, Jamshoro, Pakistan
bDepartment of Medicine, Jinnah Sindh Medical University, Karachi, Pakistan
cSchool of Medicine, The Maldives National University, Malé, Maldives
dPublic Health, Organisation Mondiale de la Santé (WHO), Antananarivo, Madagascar

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Aminath Waafira wafiagany@gmail.com Boîte postale 362, Antananarivo 101, Madagascar.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a leading global cause of death. While primary Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) vaccination offers protection in children, the role of BCG revaccination in adults remains unclear. This study evaluated the efficacy, immunogenicity, and safety of BCG revaccination against TB. A comprehensive literature search through July 2025, with independent data extraction by two reviewers. RevMan version 5.4 was used for analysis by applying random-effects models with 95% CIs. Six RCTs, involving 2400 participants, showed no significant effect of BCG revaccination on initial (RR 0.99) or sustained (RR 0.80) QFT conversion, showing only modest effectiveness against MTB. Immunogenicity evaluation showed a moderate increase in CD8+ T cell (p = .01) and cytokine-producing CD4+ T cell responses (p = .008). Safety analysis indicated increased adverse events, though a reduced risk of upper respiratory tract infections (RR 0.37) was noted. These results highlight a discrepancy between immune activation and quantifiable protection, suggesting limited BCG revaccination benefit in high-risk groups and the need for vaccines with proven protective immunity.

funding-group.funding-statement: The author(s) reported there is no funding associated with the work featured in this article.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Tuberculosis (TB) remains one of the leading causes of mortality linked to a single infectious pathogen worldwide. It is estimated that approximately one-fourth of the global population has developed an immune response to Mycobacterium Tuberculosis despite lacking any clinical manifestations, microbiological confirmation, or radiological evidence of active TB disease.1 In 2022, there were approximately 10.6 million people diagnosed with TB cases globally, which includes 1.3 million children younger than 15 y.2 Still, recent advancements in screening, preventive interventions, and clinical management have drastically reduced its burden.3 The majority of TB cases occur in lower to middle-class countries,4,5 and the disease is most effectively managed with prolonged, multi-drug treatment with therapeutic protocols based on current evidence.

The Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) vaccine, a non-virulent variant of Mycobacterium bovis developed in 1921 by Albert Calmette and Camille Guérin in France, is administered primarily to newborns as a single dose for protection against TB. Its main preventive effect is to limit bacillary dissemination from the primary infection site. However, reported protective efficacy varies considerably, ranging from about 80% to minimal benefit depending on differences in methods, vaccine strain variations, and high prevalence of nontuberculous mycobacteria.6 For adults, who contribute more significantly to TB transmission, the vaccine’s protective effect is variable.7 Variability in efficacy among BCG substrains is believed to be linked with genetic differences. The original M. bovis isolate, obtained from cattle, underwent 230 serial passages on glycerinated potato bile medium to yield the non-virulent BCG strain.8 Modern genomic sequencing has identified 16 distinct regions of difference (RDs) across BCG sub-strains present worldwide.9 Evidence suggests that these strain-specific differences influence the immunological responses induced, with some studies linking certain sub-strains to differential long-term TB resistance. Infants and young children, whose immune systems undergo substantial ontogenetic changes in the first 2 y of life, exhibit innate and adaptive immune limitations that may shape outcomes from following infection or vaccination.10

BCG revaccination in adults can modulate CD4+ T-cell phenotypes, contributing to protective immunity against MTB.11 Considering the limited availability of alternative TB vaccines, assessing the potential of repeated BCG administration remains a key area of research priority. Prolonged follow-up studies after revaccination may uncover patterns associated with the design and evaluation of future mycobacterial vaccines. Several randomized controlled trials (RCTs) have assessed the impact of BCG revaccination on TB infection rates, immunological markers, and treatment outcomes, often in comparison to placebo or alternative control interventions. Certain studies indicate a decrease in persistent M. tuberculosis infection among specific populations.12 Immunity-related outcome measures such as T-cell subset activation and cytokine production have been investigated in these trials, offering insight into the biological mechanisms of BCG’s potential benefits.

Despite its widespread use, BCG vaccination does not guarantee protection against TB infection. The vaccine’s efficacy remains inadequate for disease control, and it does not prevent primary infection or reactivation of latent pulmonary TB.13

The primary objective of this meta-analysis is to evaluate BCG revaccination as a means to enhance immunological response in previously vaccinated patients and reduce susceptibility to subsequent Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection, rather than as a treatment intervention for active tuberculosis. Although several of the trials included BCG in persons with prior exposure or latent infection, the major goal of these investigations was immune boosting or future prevention rather than treatment of established infections. Secondary objectives include assessing the incidence of adverse effects and exploring variations among subgroups based on age, region, and other demographic factors.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) criteria.14 This study is registered with PROSPERO (CRD420251102242) accessible at https://www.crd.york.ac.uk/PROSPERO/view/CRD420251102242.

sec.title: Data sources and search strategy
sec.p: This meta-analysis was conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Review and Meta-analysis (PRISMA) criteria. An extensive search was conducted in July 2025 on certain databases, which included PubMed, Google Scholar, Scopus, and Cochrane Library. Further search of clinical trials was performed on ClinicalTrials.gov. Two objective authors (SHA and SS) searched without restrictions or conflicts of interest. The following search items were used: [mycobacterium tuberculosis AND (BCG revaccination OR mycobacterium bovis) AND placebo AND (treatment outcomes OR adverse effects)].

sec.title: Study selection and eligibility criteria
sec.p: We only included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) or cluster RCTs, comparing BCG revaccination (as monotherapy) against control (placebo/pre-isoniazid therapy), evaluating BCG revaccination as an immunomodulatory or preventive intervention in previously vaccinated individuals without active infection, including disease-free participants or those with latent infection. We have included studies published between 2014 and 2025. For trials that have a crossover design, only data from the first randomization period will be considered due to concerns over carryover effects. We did not include studies that provide reviews of trials that will be published independently or uncontrolled trials. Clinical trials that meet these criteria, i.e., do not involve participants who haven’t taken BCG vaccination prior, and do not include pharmacological intervention as monotherapy (BCG revaccination), combine these interventions with other immunotherapies that might confound the results, or have fewer than 10 patients per treatment group, were not included. We also excluded any kind of observational studies. Peer-reviewed articles, commentaries, letters to the editor, and case reports would be excluded.

sec.title: PICO framework and data extraction
sec.p: Data was extracted according to the PICO framework as per PRISMA guidelines. The participants included the population who had received prior BCG vaccination. The intervention included BCG revaccination as monotherapy, while the comparator included control (placebo/INH). The outcomes included QFT conversion test for MTB infection, immunological response, and safety analysis, mainly including adverse effects. The retrieved articles were exported to Rayyan.ai,15 where, first, duplicate articles were removed from the list. Next, two reviewers (SIA and MS) carefully evaluated the remaining articles, and only those that satisfied the aforementioned eligibility criteria were included. The first screening process was based on title and abstract. A subsequent screening process was performed on the remaining articles, based on their full texts. A third facilitator (SS) was approached to settle any conflicts regarding the results. Data for baseline characteristics and outcomes, retrieved from the completed RCTs, were used to create an online Google Spreadsheet. Baseline characteristics included mean age (SD), gender, body weight, race, and baseline BMI. All analyses, including harmonization, were carried out with summary-level data collected from published publications and supplementary materials. Individual participant-level raw data were not accessed or used. Only when comparable outcomes, measurement methods, and follow-up duration were used to characterize the results were they combined. Results were either examined independently or removed from quantitative analysis when there were significant variations. The outcomes included were:

sec.title: Primary outcomes
sec.p:
1- The efficacy of the intervention (BCG Revaccination) will be measured by QFT conversion assessed at 6 months post-revaccination, which includes:
Initial QFT conversion: An interferon-γ value of 0.35 IU/mL or more because of the QuantiFERON test result within the follow-up period.16
Sustained QFT conversion: Continuous positive results (interferon-γ ≥0.35 IU/mL) without reversion to negative 6 months after initial QFT conversion.16

2- The immunogenicity of the intervention (BCG revaccination) will be measured by the magnitude and type of immune response, which may be innate or adaptive, and includes the following:
NK cells (CD56) response: The quantitative and functional activation of CD56+ CD3− Natural Killer (NK) lymphocytes, measured by degranulation (CD107a), cytokine production (IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-10), and morphological changes following stimulation through activating receptors (CD16, NCRs, and NKG2D) and cytokine pre-activation.17
γδ T-cell response: The quantitative and functional activation of γδ T or TCR γ+ δ+ CD3+ lymphocytes resulting in cytokine production (e.g., IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-17), direct cytotoxic activity against target cells, and stimulation of adaptive immune cells (e.g., T and B cells) following receptor activation via TCR-dependent or NK receptor-dependent pathways.18
NKT-like cell response: The rapid activation of natural killer T lymphocytes, identified by their αβ TCR and NK-like surface markers, leading to the production of multiple cytokines (including Th1, Th2, and Th17 types) and chemokines, upregulation of activation markers, and modulation of downstream immune cell responses after stimulation with CD1d-presented lipid antigens.19
Th1 CD4+ T-cells (IL-2/IFN-γ/TNF) response: The activation of CD4+ T helper lymphocytes is characterized by the production of Th1-associated cytokines, including interleukin-2 (IL-2), interferon-γ (IFN-γ), and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), involved in cell-mediated immunity responses.20
Th17/Th22 CD4+ T-cells (IL-17/IL-22): Th17/Th22 CD4+ T-cell responses are characterized by their production of effector cytokines IL-17 and IL-22 (Th17) and IL-22 (Th22), respectively, thereby contributing to proinflammatory and protective functions based on the type of inflammatory response.21
CD8+ T-cell responses (IFN-γ/TNF): Functional activation of CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes, assessed by MHC-I antigen-induced cytokine production, interferon-γ, and tumor necrosis factor-α, and expression of cytolytic molecules such as perforin and granzyme B.22
Any Cytokines producing CD4+ T-cell response: Generation and detection of CD4+ T lymphocytes and multiple subsets like Th1, Th2, Th17, Th22, Th9, regulatory T cells, followed by production of immune-modulatory cytokines, including but not limited to IFN-γ, IL-2, TNF-α, IL-4, IL-5, IL-13, IL-17, and IL-22.23

Others are,
MAITs cell response: Activation and effector activity of mucosal-associated invariant T (MAIT) cells are identified by their semi-invariant TCR (e.g., Vα7.2‑Jα33/12/20 in humans) and microbial riboflavin metabolites presented by MR1 molecules, leading to the production of cytokines like IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-17 for antimicrobial defense.24
Monocytes TNF-alpha response: Activity of monocytes is measured by their production of specific cytokines like TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1α, IL-1β, or surface marker expression, in response to a defined stimulus (e.g., lipopolysaccharide or LPS).25
Poly-functional T-cell response: The ability of individual T cells to concurrently perform multiple effector functions, like production of two or more cytokines/effector markers (e.g., IFN-γ, IL-2, TNF-α, degranulation markers) upon defined stimulation, is quantified at the single‑cell level using assays such as multiparameter flow cytometry.26

3- The major adverse effects of the intervention (BCG Revaccination) include:
Solicited AEs
Unsolicited AEs
Local AEs, which mainly include injection site pain, injection site erythema, and injection site ulcer
Systemic AEs, which mainly include headache, fatigue, pyrexia, and upper respiratory tract infections (URTI)

Others include any AE, mild AE, moderate AE, severe AE, SAE, SAE leading to death, related AE, injection site swelling, injection site induration, injection site ulcer, myalgia, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy, chills, stomach problems, and oropharyngeal pain.

sec.title: Study the risk of bias assessment
sec.p: The modified Cochrane Collaboration risk of bias technique27 was utilized to check the reliability of RCTs. Quality assessment and risk of bias were independently assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool 2 on all included RCTs by two investigators, and the results were matched. Any discrepancies were resolved by team consensus. Randomization bias, departures from planned interventions, missing outcome data, outcome assessment, and selective reporting were among the domains evaluated.

The Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 (RoB 2) method was used to analyze bias across five specific domains: the randomization process, variations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, measurement of the outcome, and the reported results selection. According to the Cochrane guidelines, each domain was classified as either low risk, moderate concerns, or high risk, with an overall risk-of-bias judgment assigned to each outcome. This technique was chosen to ensure a transparent assessment across all the included randomized controlled trials.

sec.title: Statistical analysis and data synthesis
sec.p: The meta-analysis was conducted using Review Manager (version 5.4. Copenhagen: Nordic Cochrane Center, The Cochrane Collaboration, 2014)28 and the forest plots were generated to visualize the results. For dichotomous outcomes, which included safety outcomes, we employed risk ratios (RRs) with 95% Confidence Intervals (CIs). For continuous outcomes, we used the inverse-variance method and reported mean differences (MDs) with 95% CIs. If the design and results of at least two investigations were sufficiently similar, a meta-analysis was planned. Variability between studies was taken into consideration using a random-effects model (DerSimonian and Laird). A narrative synthesis was offered in cases when meta-analysis was impractical. The I2 statistic was used to quantify statistical heterogeneity, with values of 25%, 50%, and 75% representing low, moderate, and high heterogeneity, respectively. Since major studies provided data in graphs for primary and secondary outcomes, Web Plot Digitizer29 was employed to extract the data in either mean ± SD or median IQR values.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: To assess publication bias, we inspected funnel plots using Comprehensive Meta-analysis software.30

sec.title: Certainty of evidence assessment
sec.p: GRADE pro31 was used to assess the certainty of the evidence for the primary and secondary outcomes. Each outcome was evaluated for risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and potential publication bias. The final certainty ratings (high, moderate, low, or very low) are reported alongside the findings.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
Through a comprehensive literature search across databases like PubMed, Cochrane, Scopus, and Google Scholar, a total of 251 possibly relevant studies were found. After discarding the 7 duplicate titles, 244 articles were left for screening the titles and abstracts. Of these, 236 were excluded since they had no connection to the study’s objectives. The remaining eight studies were assessed closely, out of which two more articles were removed as they failed to satisfy the review’s comparison requirements. In the end, six RCTs were included in the final analysis after meeting the inclusion criteria, as shown in Figure 1. Figure 1.
PRISMA flow diagram.

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
Table 1 shows the baseline demographic characteristics from six RCTs, conducted between 2014 and 2025, distributed within intervention and control groups. The total population ranged from 22 to 1835 participants, including adolescents and young adults, with mean ages from 13.5 to 27 y. The intervention in all studies involved BCG revaccination, with two trials in combination with isoniazid (INH), while control groups received either a placebo or combinations (INH + BCG). Sex distribution was relatively equal, particularly in larger trials, with near-balanced male-to-female ratios. Body mass index (BMI) ranged from approximately 19.5 to 26 kg/m2 in the intervention group and 19.78 to 26 kg/m2 in the control group. Race and ethnicity were addressed in larger studies, such as those by E Nemes (2018) and Alexander (2025), which shows majority representation from Black (126/120 and 729/735) and Mixed (200/207 and 125/120) racial groups and minority from the White population, and Virginie (2020) reports Asian population (3/2), whereas smaller trials (Priya 2022, Sara 2016, Mark 2014) did not report any racial profile.Table 1.
Baseline characteristics.
Author & YearStudy DesignTotal PopulationDuration of BCG vaccination before revaccinationStudy ArmsPopulationAge, Mean ± SDM/F (n)BMI Mean ± SDRace Distribution
Priya A Debisarun 202221RCTn = 223 monthsIntervention (BCG)(n = 17)24.5 ± 7.64/1322.9 ± 2.5Not Reported
Control (placebo)(n = 5)22.8 ± 3.11/421.9 ± 3.9Not Reported
E. Nemes 201813Trialn = 989At BirthIntervention (BCG)(n = 330)14.25 ± 0.863168/16219.50 ± 4.11White: 3, Black: 126, Asian: 1, Mixed: 200
Control (placebo)(n = 329)14.25 ± 0.864160/16919.78 ± 3.89White: 1, Black: 120, Asian: 1, Mixed: 207
Alexander C 202522RCTn = 1835≥5 yIntervention (BCG)(n = 918)13.5 ± 1.249444/47423.95 ± 4.81White: 1, Black: 729, Mixed: 125, Others: 63
Control (placebo)(n = 917)13.5 ± 1.249448/46923.65 ± 4.72White: 0, Black: 735, Mixed: 120, Others: 61
Virginie Rozot 202023Trialn = 52At BirthIntervention (BCG)(n = 28)14 ± 0.99416/1220.75 ± 2.91Asian: 3, Others: 25
Control (placebo)(n = 24)15 ± 1.02712/1220.28 ± 2.16Asian: 2, Others: 22
Sara Suliman 201624RCTn = 72At BirthIntervention (INH after BCG)(n = 39)27 ± 614/2526 ± 6Not Reported
Control (INH before BCG)(n = 33)26 ± 610/2326 ± 6Not Reported
Mark Hatherill 201425RCTn = 82At BirthIntervention (INH after BCG)(n = 40)27 ± 614/2626 ± 6Not Reported
Control (INH before BCG)(n = 42)26 ± 613/2926 ± 6Not Reported

The included trials varied in terms of population size, age distribution, and geographic location, which were specifically considered when synthesizing the data by restricting the pooling to results with similar definitions and measurement techniques and by applying random-effects models to account for between-study heterogeneity.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p:
The quality of individual studies was assessed in accordance with the study design using the Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool 2,27 which was employed to evaluate the risk of bias in each study. Among the six included studies, four (Mark 2014, Virginie 2020, Alexander C 2025, and Priya A 2022) have been found to have a high overall risk of bias, with some concerns in Domain 2 in the Mark 2014 study. However, two studies (Sara 2016 and E Nemes 2018) had a low risk ratio throughout all domains, whereas all studies were found to have a low risk in Domains 1 and 3, as shown in Figure 2. Figure 2.
Risk of bias assessment.

sec.title: Efficacy
sec.p: According to QFT conversion, BCG revaccination did not provide substantial protection against Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection in the included trials. Both initial and persistent QFT outcomes had non-significant effect estimates, showing that revaccination has a poor value in infection prevention.

Figure 3 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on Initial QFT conversion. The pooled analysis from two studies shows a non-significant effect of BCG revaccination with a pooled risk ratio of 0.99 (95% CI 0.80–1.21, p = .90), indicating only 1% efficacy against Mtb infection. There is very low heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 8%), showing consistent results across studies. Figure 3.
Initial QFT conversion.

Figure 4 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on sustained QFT conversion, the pooled analysis from two studies shows non-significant effect of BCG revaccination with a pooled risk ratio of 0.80 (95% CI 0.46–1.39, p = .42), indicating 20% efficacy against Mtb infection. There is moderate heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 69%), showing some variability in results across studies.Figure 4.
Sustained QFT conversion.

sec.title: Innate immunogenicity
sec.p: The innate immune responses to BCG revaccination were variable and extremely diverse among trials. The pooled effects of NK cell, γδ T cell, and NKT-like cell responses were not statistically significant, and there was significant diversity between studies.

Figure 5 shows a forest plot comparing the effect of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on the NK cell (CD56) response. The pooled analysis from two studies shows a non-significant increase in the NK cell response with a pooled mean difference of 3.65 (95% CI −2.67 to 9.97, p = .26), indicating that BCG revaccination enhances the NK cell (CD56) response, although the results are not statistically significant. There is high heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 81%), showing inconsistent results across studies. Figure 5.
NK cell (CD56) response.

Figure 6 shows a forest plot comparing the effect of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on the γδ T cell response. The pooled analysis from two studies shows no significant effect of BCG revaccination with a pooled mean difference of −0.23 (95% CI −3.72 to 3.25, p = .89), indicating that BCG revaccination does not significantly enhance the γδ T cell response. Figure 6.
γδ T cell response.

Figure 7 shows a forest plot comparing the effect of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on the NKT-like cell response. The pooled analysis from two studies shows a non-significant increase in the NKT-like cell response with a pooled mean difference of 2.18 (95% CI −5.53 to 9.88, p = .58), indicating that BCG revaccination enhances the NKT-like cell response, although the results are not statistically significant. There is high heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 92%), showing inconsistent results across studies.Figure 7.
NKT-like cell response.

sec.title: Adaptive immunogenicity
sec.p: Unlike innate immunity, BCG revaccination strengthened selected adaptive immune responses. CD8+ T cell responses and cytokine-producing CD4+ T cells showed significant increases with little variability, indicating a consistent adaptive immune boosting effect.

Figure 8 shows a forest plot comparing the effect of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the Th1 CD4+ T cell response. The pooled analysis from two studies shows no significant effect of BCG revaccination with a pooled mean difference of 0.71 (95% CI −0.78 to 2.21, p = .35), indicating that BCG revaccination does not significantly enhance the Th1 CD4+ T cell response. Heterogeneity between the studies is high (I2 = 89%), showing inconsistent results across the studies. Figure 8.
Th1 CD4+ T cells response (IL-2/IFN-γ/TNF) (post 6 months).

Figure 9 shows a forest plot comparing the effect of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the Th17/Th22 CD4+ T cell response. The pooled analysis from two studies shows no significant effect of BCG revaccination with a pooled mean difference of −0.24 (95% CI −1.15 to 0.67, p = .61), indicating that BCG revaccination does not significantly enhance the Th17/Th22 CD4+ T cell response. Heterogeneity between the studies is high (I2 = 96%), showing inconsistent results across the studies. Figure 9.
Th17/Th22 CD4+ T cells response (IL-17/IL-22).

Figure 10 shows a forest plot comparing the effect of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on the CD8+ T cell response. The pooled analysis from three studies shows a statistically significant effect of BCG revaccination with a pooled mean difference of 0.02 (95% CI 0.00 to 0.04, p = .01), indicating that BCG revaccination slightly enhances the CD8+ T cell response. There is no heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 0%), showing consistent results across the studies. Figure 10.
CD8+ T-cell responses (IFN-γ/TNF).

Figure 11 shows a forest plot comparing the effect of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on the cytokines producing CD4+ T cell response. The pooled analysis from three studies shows a statistically significant effect of BCG revaccination with a pooled mean difference of 0.26 (95% CI 0.07 to 0.46, p = .008), indicating that BCG revaccination moderately enhances the cytokine-producing CD4+ T cell response. There is no heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 0%), showing consistent results across the studies.Figure 11.
Cytokines producing CD4+ T cell response.

sec.title: Safety evaluation
sec.p: BCG revaccination was linked to an increase in the frequency of both local and systemic adverse effects, primarily injection-site responses and pyrexia. While most adverse events were mild to moderate in severity, significant heterogeneity among studies limits the precise risk estimates.

Figure 12 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the incidence of solicited adverse events. The pooled analysis from two studies shows non-significant increase in the incidence of solicited AEs with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 3.12 (95% CI 0.82–11.84, p = .09), indicating that there is about three times more risk of solicited AEs in participants receiving BCG revaccination There is high heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 99%) showing inconsistent results in studies. Figure 12.
Solicited AE.

Figure 13 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the incidence of unsolicited AEs. The pooled analysis from two studies shows a significant increase in the incidence of unsolicited AEs with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 2.38 (95% CI 1.07–5.33, p = .03), indicating an increased risk of unsolicited AEs in participants receiving BCG revaccination. There is high heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 97%), showing inconsistent results in the studies. Figure 13.
Unsolicited AE.

Figure 14 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on injection site pain. The pooled analysis from three studies shows a non-significant increase in injection site pain with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 2.00 (95% CI 0.81–4.97, p = .03), indicating that participants receiving BCG revaccination are 2 times more likely to experience injection site pain. There is variable heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 67%), showing promising results in the studies. Figure 14.
Injection site pain.

Figure 15 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on injection site erythema. The pooled analysis from three studies shows a statistically significant increase in injection site erythema with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 4.08 (95% CI 1.35–12.32, p = .01), indicating that participants receiving BCG revaccination are four times more likely to experience injection site erythema. There is high heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 97%), showing inconsistent results in the studies. Figure 15.
Injection site erythema.

Figure 16 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo/isoniazid pre-treatment on injection site ulcer. The pooled analysis from three studies shows a non-significant increase in injection site ulcer with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 17.50 (95% CI 0.47–646.75, p = .12), indicating that participants receiving BCG revaccination are more likely to experience injection site ulcer. There was variable heterogeneity among the included studies (I2 = 90%), limiting the certainty of the pooled result. Figure 16.
Injection site ulcer.

Figure 17 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the occurrence of headache. The pooled analysis from the two included studies shows a non-significant increase in the risk of headache with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 1.17 (95% CI 0.81–1.70, p = .38), indicating that participants receiving BCG revaccination were 1.17 times more likely to report headache compared to those receiving placebo. There was no observed heterogeneity among the studies (I2 = 0%), suggesting consistency in the findings across studies. Figure 17.
Headache.

Figure 18 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the incidence of fatigue. The pooled analysis from two studies shows a non-significant decrease in the incidence of fatigue with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 0.91 (95% CI 0.42–1.97, p = .80), indicating slightly less risk of fatigue in participants receiving BCG revaccination. There is variable heterogeneity between the studies (I2 = 76%), showing promising results in the studies. Figure 18.
Fatigue.

Figure 19 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the incidence of pyrexia. The pooled analysis from two studies shows a significant increase in the risk of pyrexia with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 3.44 (95% CI 1.13–10.49, p = .03), indicating that participants were about three times more likely to experience pyrexia receiving BCG revaccination compared to those receiving a placebo. There was no heterogeneity observed between the studies (I2 = 0%), showing encouraging results. Figure 19.
Pyrexia.

Figure 20 shows a forest plot comparing the impact of BCG revaccination versus placebo on the incidence of URTI. The analysis from two studies indicates a significant decrease in the risk of URTI with a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 0.37 (95% CI 0.19–0.73, p = .004), indicating that participants receiving BCG revaccination were much less likely to experience URTI compared to those receiving a placebo. There was low heterogeneity among the studies (I2 = 22%), suggesting reasonably consistent findings across studies.Figure 20.
Upper respiratory tract infection (URTI).

sec.title: Publication of bias
sec.p: Egger’s regression test and funnel plots were used to evaluate publication bias.30 The funnel plot for cytokines producing CD4+ T cell response appeared symmetrical, and all included studies were within the pseudo 95% confidence limits as shown in supplementary figure S1, with an Egger’s intercept value of 1.22 (p = .50), showing no statistically significant validation of publication bias. For CD8+ T-cell responses (IFN-γ/TNF), visual inspection of the funnel plot in supplementary figure S3 demonstrated symmetry, and Egger’s regression test was non-significant (intercept = 0.10, p = .96), indicating no evidence of publication bias.

sec.title: Data synthesis
sec.p:
In the six RCTs, outcomes like immunogenicity and adverse effects of BCG revaccination (intervention) and placebo groups (control) were reported. Two studies (Priya A, 2022; Virginie, 2020) assessed innate and adaptive immune responses, including monocyte TNF-α expression and polyfunctional T-cell activity. Priya A (2022) reported minimal changes in TNF-α response between BCG and placebo groups, while Rozot observed modest increases in MAIT cell and polyfunctional CD4+ T cell responses post-revaccination, as shown in Table 2. The data for adverse effects were reported across larger trials (Nemes, 2018; Alexander, 2025), with the BCG groups showing higher frequencies of any adverse event, local solicited adverse effects, and systemic complaints compared to placebo. Local adverse effects included injection site swelling, induration, and scar formation, while systemic adverse effects such as myalgia, arthralgia, stomach discomfort, and oropharyngeal pain were also noted. Two studies (Sara, 2016; Mark, 2014) provided limited immunogenicity data in the extraction, though Mark (2014) reported local effects like injection site swelling and induration as shown in Table 3.Table 2.
Immunogenicity evaluation.
AUTHORPriya A DebisarunE NemesAlexander CVirginie RozotSara SulimanMark Hatherill

sec.title: Certainty of evidence
sec.p: A summary of findings supplementary table 1 shows evidence on the effectiveness, safety, and immunogenicity of BCG revaccination versus placebo in treating Mtb.

sec.title: Efficacy
sec.p: The results indicate that BCG revaccination has a little to no effect on efficacy both initially (RR = 0.99, moderate certainty evidence, downgraded due to imprecision, as the CI included both benefit and harm) and sustained (RR = 0.80, low certainty evidence, downgraded due to substantial heterogeneity (I2 = 69%) and imprecision, with a wide CI).

sec.title: Immunogenicity
sec.p: BCG revaccination may increase NK (CD56) cell response (MD = 3.65), NKT-like cells (MD = 2.18), Th1 CD4+ T cells (IL-2/IFN-γ/TNF) (MD = 0.71), and CD8+ T-cell response (IFN-γ/TNF) (MD = 0.02; moderate certainty). It may also slightly reduce γδ T cells (MD = −0.23) and Th17/Th22 CD4+ T cells (MD = −0.24). All immunogenicity outcomes are based on low certainty evidence due to wide CIs and high heterogeneity, except cytokine-producing CD4+ T cells, which show a small increase (MD = 0.26; high certainty).

sec.title: Solicited adverse effects
sec.p: BCG revaccination may increase solicited adverse effects (RR = 3.12), including injection site pain (RR = 2.00), erythema (RR = 4.08), and ulcers (RR = 17.50). However, all outcomes are based on low-certainty evidence due to very high heterogeneity (I2 67–99%) and wide confidence intervals.

sec.title: Unsolicited adverse effects
sec.p: BCG revaccination likely increases the risk of unsolicited adverse events (RR = 2.38) and pyrexia (RR = 3.44), both with moderate certainty. It probably increases the risk of headache (RR = 1.17; moderate certainty), though the CI includes the possibility of no effect. The evidence for fatigue is uncertain (RR = 0.91; low certainty), with wide and conflicting estimates across studies. Importantly, BCG revaccination significantly reduces URTI (RR = 0.37; high certainty), showing consistent effects with low heterogeneity and a meaningful clinical benefit.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: TB is one of the life-threatening worldwide health concerns, particularly in low- and middle-income countries.32,33 Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) vaccine in children has previously provided some protection against severe forms of TB, though its protective effectiveness in pulmonary TB and the significance of BCG revaccination in adolescents and adults remain uncertain.34 BCG revaccination has recently reemerged for its potential to increase innate and adaptive immunity and provide heterologous protection, notably against respiratory diseases like COVID-19.35 The objective of this meta-analysis is to compare the safety, immunogenicity, and effectiveness of BCG revaccination to placebo or isoniazid-pretreated controls in six RCTs, involving more than 2400 people.

The pooled analysis of primary outcomes revealed that BCG revaccination offered no statistically significant protection against Mtb infection.36,37 Initial QuantiFERON-TB (QFT) conversion showed a pooled risk ratio (RR) of 0.99 (95% CI 0.80–1.21), while sustained QFT conversion showed an RR of 0.80 (95% CI 0.46–1.39) with moderate and low certainty, respectively. These findings are consistent with a previous study conducted by Auguste et al. 38 indicating that BCG revaccination has minimal effectiveness in preventing TB.39 Subgroup analysis reported minimal heterogeneity in initial QFT results (I2 = 8%) but moderate heterogeneity in sustained conversion (I2 = 69%). Certainty of evidence was reduced due to its imprecision and inconsistency. The lack of considerable protective efficacy challenges the previous observational data, which highlights the complexity of TB immunity.37,40

Regarding immunogenicity, main adaptive immune responses including CD8+ T cell responses (MD = 0.02; p = .01) and cytokine-producing CD4+ T cells responses (MD = 0.26; p = .008) showed statistically significant results, with moderate-to-high certainty. These findings are consistent with Suliman et al., which indicates that after BCG revaccination, CD8+ T cells produce several cytokines, such as IFN-γ, which are essential for a strong immune response against TB.41 Additionally, a study by Rakshit S. et al. found that BCG revaccination increases the production of several cytokines (such as IL-2 and IFN-γ) by CD4+ T cells, which improves their capacity to fight against MTB.42 Innate immune responses, including NK cell (CD56) and NKT-like cell activation, showed non-significant increases with low certainty and high heterogeneity (I2 > 80%).43 Following BCG revaccination, the frequency of BCG-reactive NK and NKT-like cells increases steadily, and the increased responses can last for up to a year, correlating with the findings from a study by Suliman et al.41 Overall, the main findings regarding the adaptive and innate immune responses, are consistent with the previous study which collectively indicate that BCG revaccination can elicit long-lasting adaptive immunological responses, including the long-lived BCG-reactive innate NK cell responses in already vaccinated individuals.41 Moreover, the innate, γδ T cell and, adaptive, Th17/Th22 responses, demonstrating negligible or slightly reduced activity post-revaccination, validate previous findings from a study by Rozot et al.44 The amplification of polyfunctional T cell responses among adaptive responses is yet unknown because of methodological heterogeneity and insufficient data.

Safety analysis indicated increased adverse events (AEs) in the BCG group.45 However, solicited AEs (RR = 3.12) and injection site erythema (RR = 4.08) showed high heterogeneity, while unsolicited AEs (RR = 2.38) and pyrexia (RR = 3.44) were significantly elevated with moderate certainty. These findings are correlated with study by Hatherill et. al. which reported that erythema and induration typified the time profile of reactogenicity at the BCG revaccination site, which peaked at 1 week. Following early erythema and induration, a tiny ulcer that peaked 2 weeks following revaccination and healed, leaving a slight scar in the majority of individuals.46 Importantly, BCG revaccination was associated with a significant reduction in URTI (RR = 0.37), a finding substantiated by studies such as Yitbarek et al. and Bourboulis et al.47,48 A study by Nemes et al. and Bannister, S et al. also reported that the rate of URTI was lower in the BCG revaccinated group than in the placebo group in the RCT of South African teenagers, which supports the idea that BCG has off-target effects.12,49 The harms–benefits ratio evaluated that though BCG revaccination may not reduce TB conversion, it might provide heterologous protection at the expense of local and systemic AEs.

Future research should prioritize long-term multicenter trials with standardized endpoints, concerning not only on TB-specific outcomes but also on broader immunological and infection-related benefits because BCG vaccine provides broader immunological and infection-related benefits beyond just TB-specific outcomes.50,51 It is necessary to identify specific biomarkers of protection and stratify participants based on prior BCG exposure and latent TB infection status as one study reported that biomarkers like T-cell activation markers, Ag85A antibodies, and IFN-γ specific T-cells can predict progression from latent TB infection to active TB disease.52 Additionally, further research should evaluate the optimal time, dosage, and population subgroups for BCG immune boosting, especially in elderly and immunocompromised patients, as reported by Işıtmangil et al. in his study that BCG vaccine may provide a more potential immune response against new pathogens 53 while Kumar et al. found that BCG vaccination indicates its potential utility in boosting immunity by enhancing memory T and B cell frequencies and dendritic cell subsets, in elderly individuals.54

sec.title: Strengths
sec.p: This study has several strengths. It integrates robust methodology that adheres to PRISMA and GRADE guidelines, an extensive synthesis of safety and immunology endpoints, and a heterogeneous sample drawn from six RCTs with varied geographic and demographic settings. Inclusion of both placebo and isoniazid pretreatment in control arms increases the generalizability of analysis. The high-certainty results on cytokine-producing T cells and lower rates of URTI are particularly noteworthy.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: However, several limitations should also be reported. Most of the included studies had a high risk of bias, especially in the domains of outcome measurement and selective reporting. Only two studies (Sara24 and E Nemes13) were rated as low risk across all domains. Small sample sizes in several immunogenicity assessments, lack of blinding, and other methodological issues may have overestimated effect sizes.55 Furthermore, high heterogeneity in most of the immunogenicity and adverse event outcomes limits the interpretability of pooled effects. Variability in BCG strain and administration protocols across studies may also contribute to heterogeneity.56

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This meta-analysis suggests that the effectiveness of BCG revaccination in preventing TB infection is limited, but it might enhance specific immune responses and lower the rates of upper respiratory tract infections. Nevertheless, it is also associated with increased risk of local and systemic AEs. These data indicate a gap between immunogenicity and clinical efficacy, emphasizing the importance of future trials focusing on validated correlations of protection rather than infection conversion alone.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: Conceptualization was carried out by SS, SIA, and SHA. Methodology was developed by UAS, MK, and MJ. Formal analysis was conducted by MSK, HS, and SHA, while MS and SS were responsible for software and data curation. The original draft was written by UAS, MJ, and MK. Review and editing of the manuscript were performed by UN, SHA, and HS. Supervision was provided by AW, UN, and SIA. All authors (SHA, SS, UAS, MK, MJ, SIA, MS, HS, MSK, UN, AW) reviewed and approved the final version of the manuscript and agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work.

bio.p: Dr. Aminath Waafira, MBBS, is currently serving as a Public Health Officer at the Organisation mondiale de la Santé (WHO), Madagascar. She received her medical degree (MBBS) from The Maldives National University - School of Medicine. Dr. Waafira previously served as a House Officer at Indira Gandhi Memorial Hospital, Maldives, where she completed her internship and initial clinical training. Her professional interests include public health, health systems strengthening, and preventive medicine.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Consent
sec.p: Informed Consent was not required for this study.

sec.title: Consent to participate
sec.p: All authors agreed to participate.

sec.title: Consent for publication
sec.p: All authors have agreed to this study.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: Ethical Approval was not required for this study.

sec.title: Supplemental material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2026.2630512
